ANALYSTS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official 
frorn rnaking,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official 
position
to influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest.
The Act provides a four-part test to determine whether a
public official 
has a conflict of interest in a governmental
-clecision. First, is the official 
considering making, participat-
ing in making, or using his official position to. influence a
qovernmental decision? (section 87100.) Second, is it
foreseeafte that the decision will affect the officialas 
econornic
int"rest=? 
(Section 87103.) Third, is the effect of the decision
on tire official's 
econonic interests material? (Id.) 
Fourth, is
the effect of the decisi,on on the offici'al's 
economic interests
distinquishable fron its effect on the DubIic qelera]]y? (Id.)
factr of tnese elements must be net before there is a conflict of
interest .
A. 
Makinq or ParticiDatinq In A Governmental Decision
An official 
makes a governmental decision when he or she
votes, comrnits his agency to a course of action, enters into a
contract, or appoints someone - 
(ReguLation 18700(b), copy
.".i"="a.1 
eaiticipating in the'rnaking of a governmental -decisons
includes, among othlr things, advising or makinq recommendations

B. 
Foreseeable Financial Effect on An Econonic Interest
The second issue is the foreseeability that the decision will
affect your economic interests' 
The parameters ot a puDrrc
;;;i;i.i;; 
econonic interest are set iorth in section 87103' For
the purposes of Your situation,
has a financial interest in a decision
rn-an:.ng of Section 87100 if it is

within the

reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, 
. . . on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment $rorth
one thousand dollars ($1,0oo) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a cornrnercial lending
institution 
in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public srithout reqard to
official 
status, aggregating .two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or pronised to the Pub1ic official
within 12 nonths prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
enployee, or hotds any position of managemeht.
***
(Section 87103. )
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be reasonably
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a nere
possibility, 
however certainty is not required. 
(Downey -cares v.
bowney Communi.ty Development Com. (1987) 196 cal . App. 3d 983,
geg-ggli Witt v. Morrot (L977) 7O Cal. App. 3d 817; In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198, copy enclosed.) The Act seeks to Prevent
nore than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even
the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(Witt v'
U.9rr9!, glpre at 823. )
Thus rrhen a matter comes before you and you suspect you may
have a conflict of interest, you should examine the natter to
deterrnine whether any decision by you would have a reasonably
foreseeable effect any of the econonic interests listed in
87103 (a) , (b) , (c) or (d) .
fn your case, three economic interests may be affected: 
your
business entity (lection 87103(a)(d)), the real property where
V"".--U".i""si is'located (Section 87103 (b), and sources.of income
t" V"". business of $250 or more within the prior 12 months
ii"'.ii"" 
87103(b)). You will need to apply the following tests to
each of these economic interests.
C.
Mater■al Financ■al Effect
The Connission
deternine whether a
has adopted a series of regulations to
ii"un"iir 
effect is naterial'

apply the regulations in a particular case, j-t is necessary to
foof at the economic interest affected and then examine both the
direct and indi,rect effects of the decision on those interests.
rf the effect of the particular decision is material under any of
the following three criteria, 
the effect is material.
1. Material Financial Effect on Real ProDertv
a. Direct Effect on Interests in Real Propertv.
The effect of a decision will be material if the decision
directlv involves real property in which the official 
has an
ownersirip interest of $1,ooo or more. Generally a decision will
directly involve the property if the decision concerns whether the
public official's 
property will be included in a zoning or
redevelopment area, a license for the use of the official's
property, or the taxes on the official's 
property. 
(Regulation
1at-oz. f , copy encLosed.) RegRrlati on L87o2.1 provides a more
comprehensive list of when a public official's 
real property is
dirLctty involved in a governmental decision.
b. Indirect Effect on fnterests in ReaL Propertv.
rf your property will not be directly affected by the
decision, you must determine whether your property nay be
indirectly affected. 
The effect of a decision will also be mate-
rial if tlre decision indirectlv involves real property in which
the official 
has an ownership interest of $1,000 or more.
Regnrlation La7o2.3, (copy enclosed) sets forth the standards
for det5rnining whether tfre inairect effects of a decision will
materially affect real property. That regulation provides in
part :
(a) The effect of a decision is material
as to real property in which an official 
has a
direct, indirect or beneficial ownership
interest (not including a leasehold interest),
if any of the following aPPIies:
(1) The real ProPerty in which the of-
ficiai 
iras an interest, or any part of that

reaL property interest '

of. or improvements to, streets, water, se$/er'

real, property in vnicn the official 
has an

has an interest is located outside a

feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision wil-l
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or
rnore on the fair market value of the real

has an inter-

period.
If the effect on your property fa1ls within either (l), 
(2),
or (3) above, the effect is araterial.
2. Material Financial Effect on Businesses
Since your real proPerty is a comrnercial property on which
you operate a business, it is aLso necessary to evaluate the
itt""t 
of the decisions on your business interests. 
Since yours
is a local business, for the purpose of this analysis we assume
that the business is not listed on any stock exchange, is not a
Fortune 5oO company, and does not have net tangible assets of at
least $4,OOO,OOO,OOO. Under these assumptions the effect of a
decision on your business is naterial if:
(1) The decision will result in an
increase or decrease in the gross revenue for
a fiscal year of $1o,oo0 or nore; or
(2') The decision rrill result in the
business entity incurring or avoiding - ad-
aitionat expenies or reducing or elininating
existing expenses for a fj-sca1 year in the
amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3) The deci.sion will result in an
increase or decrease in the value of assets or
Iiabilities 
of $10,000 or more.
(Regulation 7a702.2 (g), copy enclosed ')

You should examine the likely financial effect of any
downtown decision on your business and determine whether the
effect meets or exceeds the above amounts.
3. Material financiaf Effect on sources of Income
Fina1ly, since a business is involved, you should remember
that if any source of income of $250 or more in the 12 months
preceding Lhe decision is directly or indirectly involved in the
decision pursuant to the tests listed above, you may have a
conflict of interest. 
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (1) , La7o2-6-)
D. 
trPublic General lvtr Exception
Even if the decision before you meets all the above elenents,
disqualification is required on1-y .if the effect upon your economic
int6rests is distinguishable from the effect upon the public
generally. 
(Section 87103.) The trpublic'r is all the persons
iesidingl owning property, or doing business in the jurisdiction
of the agency in question. (fn re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC oPS. 1, 15,
copy enclosed.l In the case of a city council, this would be the
entire city. 
If the decision does not affect all the members of
the public in the same nanner, disqualification may be required
unle-ss the effect of the decision is the same as the effect on a
significant segrment of the public. 
(Regulation 18703, .coPy
en6losed.) Therefore for the public generally exception to apply,
any decision would have to affect a significant segrment of the
cii:y of Et Segundo in substantially the same manner as it would
aitlct you. (Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice
